---
title: "14: Cluster Analysis"
subtitle: "Task I"
---


::: {.cell}

:::


## Practice Task

Work through these exercises after reading the [Cluster Analysis](../cluster_analysis.qmd) chapter, using the WHO SDG data from the chapter and, for one exercise, the Doubs fish data. Four exercises are hands-on calculations and two are short conceptual questions.

1. On the WHO SDG data, compute a distance matrix and run `pam()` for k = 4, 5, and 6 clusters; show how the cluster membership changes.

2. Determine the optimal number of clusters with the silhouette and gap statistics (`factoextra::fviz_nbclust()`). What value of k do they suggest?

3. Repeat the clustering with `hclust()` (Ward linkage) and `kmeans()`, and compare the memberships with `pam()`. Are the results markedly different, and which method do you proceed with?

4. Apply hierarchical clustering to the Doubs fish data (Bray-Curtis dissimilarity, Ward linkage), cut the dendrogram into clusters, and relate the resulting site groups to the upstream-downstream river gradient.

5. Explain the difference between partitioning methods (`pam`, k-means) and hierarchical clustering, and how the linkage choice (single, complete, average, Ward) shapes the dendrogram.

6. Describe the SDG cluster patterns: how does South Africa fare relative to comparator countries of your choice, and what socio-economic explanations might underlie the patterns you see globally and regionally?

## Assessment Criteria

This Task is not formally assessed. It is built around four hands-on analyses (Exercises 1--4) and two short conceptual questions (Exercises 5--6); work through all six and bring your annotated Quarto document to class for discussion.
